title-group.article-title: The comparative advantage of urban goat production

aff.named-content: College of Natural and Computational Science

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceTakele Taye Desta, Department of Biology, College of Natural and Computational Science, Kotebe University of Education, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. Email: takele_taye@yahoo.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Urban agriculture significantly contributes to food security. The two primary components of urban agriculture are livestock production and horticulture. The goat, Capra hircus, is one of the commonly raised food animals. Goats can be used to generate income, produce milk, meat, skins, furs (hairs) and manure and provide various sociocultural and ecological services.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: This study evaluates the significance of urban goat production and recommends ways to lessen the adverse impacts of urban goat production.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This report involved an in‐depth interview with seven key informants in Adama and Addis Ababa cities.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Goats can thrive in limited urban open spaces, scavenge leftovers from homes and open markets and browse on open public land. Goats can be incorporated into urban agriculture, in so doing contributing to a circular economy. Goats can thrive on a limited supply of water and feed and require less care and space. Goat farming is used to mitigate the adverse impact of climate change. Goats are naturally active, which makes them better at avoiding traffic accidents. Goats can be used to control bush encroachment. Goat farming in cities improves land use efficiency and food security. Being friendly animals, goats can be utilized to play with kids, and they can be a basic piece of metropolitan ecotourism. However, goats can harm urban green spaces; therefore, to avoid issues of this kind, goat production must be zoned.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Urban goat farming could add a new dimension to urban food security. Extensive pieces of empirical evidence need to be generated to enhance the adoption of urban goat farming.

abstract.p: The hardy goats have a wide range of adaptations, including the urban environment. They can be significantly integrated into urban crop production and greenery management. Goat production can be achieved using limited urban spaces, and goats are clever enough to thrive in urban environments.

p.mixed-citation: Desta, T. T. (2024). The comparative advantage of urban goat production. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1473. 10.1002/vms3.1473

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Urban agriculture is gaining importance worldwide, despite not being comparable to rural agriculture (Food and Agriculture Organization [FAO], 2007). According to Duchemin et al. (2008), urban agriculture and its subfields account for 15% of global food production. According to Gu et al. (2021), sub‐Saharan Africa's population is expected to double by 2050, reaching 9.7 billion people worldwide. The efficient use of limited resources like land, water, crops and livestock is important to realize the demand for population growth, enhanced quality of life and increased purchasing power. It is worthwhile to sustainably intensify small‐scale crop and livestock production as well as devise strategies for the widespread adoption of precision agriculture to make the most of local expertise and resources. Metropolitan agribusiness fundamentally adds to food security, especially in developing nations that are encountering rapid urbanization population growth, and rising income (International Fund for Agricultural Development [IFAD], 2020).

Raising livestock is the core of urban agriculture. The goat, Capra hircus, is a common food animal (Panth et al., 2021; Utaaker et al., 2021) and is a member of the Bovidae family; however, it typically falls under the category of small ruminants. The goat has a long breeding and management history, and probably it was tamed quite a while back in the eastern part of Asia Minor (Reed, 1974). Goats have a wide range of ecological adaptations and offer various products and services. The goat is kept in diverse environments, ranging from arid to alpine (Utaaker et al., 2021). Goats may have thrived in arid and semi‐arid environments due to their naturally active nature, water use efficiency and browsing habits (Duchemin et al., 2008). According to FAO (2018), there are roughly 1 billion goats across the world. However, most of these goats are kept by small‐scale farmers in developing nations. According to IFAD (2020), agricultural systems that produce more per unit area of land are highly sought after in urban areas, where land is a limiting production input and there is a high demand for livestock products.

Innovative technologies and tools must be developed to enhance the expansion of urban agriculture and its output. The prudent application of environmentally friendly, financially viable and affordable technologies is vital for the sustainability of urban agriculture. In this study, the significance of urban goat farming is evaluated, and it also makes recommendations to lessen the adverse impacts of urban goat production.

sec.title: The study sites
sec.p: The cities of Addis Ababa and Adama are found in Ethiopia's central highlands and central Rift Valley, respectively. Adama is roughly located at 8° 30′ 52.1172″ N latitude and 39° 16′ 9.3252″ E longitude, whereas Addis Ababa is approximately found at 9° 0′ 19.4436″ N latitude and 38° 45′ 48.9996″ E longitude. In Adama, small‐ and medium‐scale cattle finishing and poultry farming are common types of metropolitan agribusiness. Dairying, apiculture, woody plant nurseries and small‐scale irrigated and wetland horticulture dominate the urban agriculture landscape of Addis Ababa and the surrounding areas.

sec.title: The study methods and organization of the report
sec.p: The exploratory observational study that was carried out in the two cities, in‐depth interviews of seven (three in Addis Ababa and four in Adama) key informants, informal discussions and personal observation formed the foundation of this report (Figure 1). The information gathered covers the advantages and disadvantages of urban goat farming, as well as the unique behaviour of goats in the urban farming system and the prevailing opportunities and threats. The findings of this qualitative study are narrated and thoroughly discussed.

caption.p: The conceptual framework of data generation methods.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: The summary of reported pros and cons of urban goat farming is presented in Figure 2. The status, opportunities and threats of urban goat farming are presented in the following sections.

caption.p: The reported pros and cons of urban goat production.

sec.title: Goats are shrewd creatures
sec.p: The respondents agreed that goats are almost certainly more intelligent than their sibling domestic animals. Goats can make the most of the limited resources in urban areas attributable to this intellect, and they are excellent explorers of their environs. Goats have become more independent and less dependent because of these behavioural attributes. Compared to companion livestock species, scavenging goats are adept at trespassing through a complex web of human and motorized traffic flow. Goats do not shy away as sheep do, so they can carry on their activities safely and rarely get into accidents that could have been avoided. When other livestock species are crossing roads in cities, most of the time they die or get hurt in traffic accidents. Goats are good at exploring their surroundings, so accidents might happen to them, but they happen so rarely. Additionally, goats respond quickly to emergencies, for instance, traffic‐related alarm calls. Goats likewise decide rapidly, which empowers them to go to great lengths against mishaps. Goats that are kept by urban producers can scavenge on roadsides (Figure 3) among construction sites (Figure 4) that other livestock species cannot access due to their clever nature. Goats are quick readers of their environments, and as a result, they quickly escape when mishaps are encountered. Because they bleat when someone new comes into the house, goats help to keep their owners’ residences safe.

caption.p: Goats were scavenging along the roadside in Adama City.
Goats were grazing on a new construction site in Addis Ababa City.

sec.title: Browsing habits of goats
sec.p: The respondents connoted that because feed resources are usually scarce in urban areas, extensive exploration, movement and the effective utilization of limited feeds are required. Goats, for instance, tend to move around more frequently than sheep do, which enables them to investigate feed resources across vast tracts of land. Even goats occasionally use their front legs to climb trees to pick up low‐lying leaves and twigs and browse. Goats can scavenge on the edges of steep hills and extremely rough terrain. Goats are adept at avoiding poisonous herbs and woody plants.

The key informants reported that goats are deft herbivores. They can graze if a few species are found for browsing. Unlike poultry, pigs and beef cattle, goats do not directly compete with humans for grains, and their feed requirements are significantly lower than those of cows. Even during the dry season, the evergreen browsing species typically contain a lot of protein, which helps the goat quickly meet their nutrient needs. To beautify the local landscape, browsing species can be established in urban areas, and goats can regularly feed on pruned twigs and leaves. Goats are selective and group scavengers who consume the most nutrient‐dense parts of the vegetation quickly. This feeding behaviour demonstrates that they require less time to satisfy their nutritional needs, which reduces flocking time and labour costs. Goats can thrive on roughages of poor quality. Goats frequently consume fruit and vegetable peels, which otherwise contribute to environmental pollution.

sec.title: The comparative advantage of urban goat farming
sec.p: All the respondents noted that goats are hardy, resilient to disease and require little care. Goats are prolific, and litter sizes are typically larger than those of sheep. Reportedly, goats have a gestation length of 5 months compared to sheep's 6 months. However, it was reported that the day open is shorter in sheep, which compensates for a longer gestation length. The cost of raising goats is lower than that of sheep. Due to its perceived medicinal value, goat's meat is gaining acceptance over mutton in Ethiopia. People have a strong belief that the meat and milk of goats may have medicinal value because they browse various plant species. Compared to starting a dairy farm in a city, goat farming is cheaper and easier. In Ethiopia, hard currency is generated from goat meat and skin. Goats’ meat is less likely to spoil quickly due to its low moisture content and can be consumed in a few meals by an average‐sized family.

sec.title: Goats’ involvement in urban landscape management
sec.p: The observational study noted goats that were scavenging on roadsides (Figure 5), as well as weeds, bushes and shrubs; as a result, they improved the scene of urban landscapes by acting as biological cleaners. Goats could easily consume the twigs and leaves of urban greenery that have been pruned, lowering the cost of disposing of waste. Goats transform resources into highly sought‐after products like milk and meat, which otherwise contribute to urban pollution. Goats can control the growth of open public swards by grazing on them. Goats may not significantly contribute to urban land degradation and soil compaction due to their small weight. It is uncommon to see livestock in urban areas; thus, the presence of radiant goats gives a conspicuous scene to the metropolitan populace and provides an opportunity for metropolitan youth and youngsters to play with them. It is practically possible to raise show goats for their aesthetic value, as companion animals, as pets or as a hobby. Goats check the growth of vegetation and, subsequently, reduce the risk of fire hazards.

caption.p: Goats are gathered in a farm field in a suburban area.

sec.title: Integrating urban goat farming with mixed crop–livestock agriculture
sec.p: According to the respondents, goats fertilize crops with manure and urine, as well as microgreens like bucket gardens, tree seedlings and urban greenery. Urban goats could feed on the leftovers of crop products that are produced along the value chain. This practice is highly desired from the perspective of supporting a circular economy, and it cascades the flow of nutrients. Chicken can also be integrated with goat production. Chickens can benefit from leftovers of goats’ feed, hatchlings and worms that are created in goat droppings, and they can pick up ectoparasites like ticks from goats. The urban livelihood system is diversified through goat farming. Goats can help with seed dispersal. Goats can be integrated with the production of other livestock.

sec.title: The economic benefits of urban goat farming
sec.p: The respondents have shared their experiences on the economic advantages of urban goat farming. Youth in urban areas and low‐income families, including women, may be able to find job opportunities through goat farming. Goats can be used as collateral for informal loans from co‐workers, and they could also act as a live bank and contribute to the creation of wealth. Due to its proximity to the terminal market and the absence of a lengthy supply chain, urban goat farming is advantageous for both producers and consumers. Due to the farms’ proximity to consumers, the transaction cost is low. Goat farming is a lucrative business with a high rate of return on investment. To increase profits, urban goat farmers must adopt semi‐intensive farming. Because it costs less to set up and keep up, urban goat farming is a low‐risk business. It enables households to consume in‐house produced fresh milk and meat. In times of crisis, such as drought or crop failure, people can consume the products of drought‐tolerant goats.

sec.title: Undesirable characteristics of urban goat farming
sec.p: Respondents reported the undesirable attributes of urban goat farming. If they are left unattended, goats can cause damage to private properties, protected areas and urban greenery (Figure 6). Goats frequently enter private lands and protected areas to scavenge, prompting a wide‐ranging conflict. Open‐market vegetables and fruits are frequently taken by goats. They grabbed the grains that were left to dry in the sun to grind them into flour and grains that were sold on open markets. Due to its goaty scent, goat's milk is rarely consumed. Free‐roaming goats are increasingly being taken while wandering, and occasionally, well‐planned, coerced robberies supported by gangster networks may occur. Due to their small weight, they are ideal targets for theft. Bankruptcy could result from inadequate knowledge of goat farming. When they were in oestrus, goats occasionally made a lot of noise, which annoyed the community. Goats, unlike sheep, are not docile, so they need fences.

sec.title: Reducing the undesirable impacts of urban goat farming
sec.p: The key informants suggested a way to reduce the undesirable features of urban goat farming. Goats may vocalize at night when they are hungry, hurt or when there is a likely threat. Does may also call to them if their kids become separated from them. Does in oestrus may vocalize more than the rest of the goats. If there is no buck around when a doe goes into heat, she may blubber and groan like a buck in a rut. Goats may not make unsettling noises if they are healthy and have all their requirements met. To lessen the negative impact of goats on urban green areas, fencing the vegetation, feeding the goats properly and employing deterrents might be utilized.

sec.title: DISCUSSIONS
sec.p: Urban agriculture emerges as an important means of subsistence as malnutrition moves from rural to urban areas (FAO, 2007). Dairy goat farming is gradually gaining popularity and importance (Tajonar et al., 2022). However, the national research system and the livestock extension advisory services have overlooked urban goat farming (Solomon et al., 2014). Small‐scale goat farming is a profitable industry with tangible benefits (IFAD, 2020; Kaumbata et al., 2020). The multi‐purpose goats provide various products and services (Abraham et., 2017; Solomon et al., 2014). Goat farming can also be integrated with mixed crop‐livestock agriculture (Abraham et al., 2017) and home garden vegetable production (Abraham et al., 2017; IFAD, 2015). According to Duchemin et al. (2008) and IFAD (2020), goat farming significantly benefits marginalized sects of the traditional community, like women. Urban goat farming is vital for reducing unemployment and poverty.

Goats have a significantly larger litter size; for example, half of them do give birth to multiple kids, and goats produce more milk than sheep do. Goat lactation lasted an average of 115 days and produced an average of 127 g of milk per day above and beyond suckling (Rath, 1992). According to Dereje (2011), Hararghe highland goats in eastern Ethiopia have a 3.6‐month lactation period and a daily average milk yield of 0.4 kg, making them more efficient milk producers on a tropical livestock unit basis than indigenous cows, which produce 2 L of milk daily. According to Jainudeen et al. (2000), goats reach puberty earlier than sheep. They also stated that the mating ratio of male to female is 1:50 in goats and 1:30 in sheep, indicating that bucks are effectively utilized.

Goat milk and meat are comparatively rich in nutrients. Goat milk, in contrast to cow milk, does not cause allergies (Park, 1994). Compared to cow and camel milk, goat's milk contains significantly more protein and fat (Legesse et al., 2017). Sheep meat is juicier than goat meat and has a higher drip loss rate than goat meat. Goat fat can be easily removed from the abdominal area because it does not accumulate in the subcutaneous region. However, the cooked cuts’ moisture, protein, fat, ash and dry matter content do not differ significantly between goats and sheep (Schönfeldt et al., 1993).

Goats will not pose a significant threat to the ecosystem if the appropriate stocking rate is adopted. Goats can be fed through cut‐and‐carry systems. According to Yilmaz et al. (2016), goats are better at climbing trees and scavenging on rocky escarpments and marginal lands (Morales et al., 2019) in comparison to their sibling domestic herbivores. In contrast to grasses, browse species do not lose much of their nutrients during the dry season, and goats compete less with cattle during browsing than grazer sheep do (Mphinyane et al., 2015). Goats are resilient to rising temperatures and drought, making them the ideal species to cope with the adverse effects of climate change (Joy et al., 2020; Nair et al., 2021).

Goats are curious and intelligent animals (D. Larsen, 2015). Goat farming requires little capital; however, according to a study conducted in India's Karnataka's Mandya and Mysuru districts, it makes more money than raising sheep (Shivakumara & Kira, 2019). Even so, this productivity could be significantly attributed to the divergence in flock size, which was high in sheep. Urban goat producers may not confront a challenge while selling their products as they are situated inside the domain of the terminal market (Panth et al., 2021; Tajonar et al., 2022). Following Icoutchika et al. (2022) findings in Benin, goats are typically kept in Ethiopia for meat production. Goat milk's unusual flavour may be a contributing factor to its low consumption rate (Icoutchika et al., 2022). However, pastoral areas of Ethiopia usually consume goat milk (Arse et al., 2013). In addition, goat milk is a product that is highly sought‐after in Sudan (Abdalla et al., 2012).

According to Arse et al. (2013) and Abraham et al. (2017), a scarcity of feed and poor veterinary services severely affect goat production in Ethiopia, which calls for specific interventions to lessen the negative effects that come with them. However, goat farming is a profitable and low‐risk venture (Abdalla et al., 2012; Solomon et al., 2014). Goats can adapt to a wide variety of agroecologies due to their tough nature and feeding habits (Rath, 1992; Utaaker et al., 2021). The woody plant species that support goat production are less affected by drought compared to herbaceous plants that are fed by sheep (Dias‐Silva & Abdalla Filho, 2020). To compare the viability of various livestock production systems in urban areas, a 2002 study by Fialor (cited in FAO, 2007) in Ghana recommended owning more than 40 goats to maximize profits.

sec.title: CONCLUSION
sec.p: In most cases, urban goat farming is a profitable business. By and by, there may be an endurable downside that should be alleviated by using locally tailored interventions. This exploratory study has produced vital information that can be used as a starting point for more detailed studies. Urban farmers and livestock extension advisory services should have a place to talk about and share their experiences with the successes and failures of goat farming. Urban livestock production systems must be governed by well‐defined guidelines. Urban goat farming could add a new dimension to urban food security. Extensive pieces of empirical evidence need to be generated to enhance the adoption of urban goat farming.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Resources; supervision; validation; visualization; writing – original draft; writing – review and editing: Takele Taye Desta.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The author declares that he does not have any other interests at stake.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: No funds were available for this study.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: All respondents have given informed verbal consent.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1473.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
ack.p: Respondents involved in the discussion are highly acknowledged for sharing their invaluable knowledge and insight.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data is already included in the manuscript
